decision_scenario: Lo! Foods is choosing where to place Protein Chef bread in modern trade retail. Option A: health foods aisle alongside functional food brands. Option B: bread aisle next to mainstream breads. Option C: dedicated branded end-cap display. Which placement makes you most likely to pick up and buy Protein Chef — and which placement would you find it in 6 months later and still consider it a premium brand worth the price?

rounds.stimulus: You're in a DMart or Spencer's. Scenario A: You're in the 'Health Foods' aisle alongside Yoga Bar, True Elements, Millet Amma, and RiteBite Max Protein. You spot Protein Chef bread at ₹99 on the shelf. The aisle has a 'Functional Foods' signboard. How does this placement feel — does it reinforce the brand's premium health positioning, or does it make it invisible among too many similar options?

Same store, different placement. Scenario B: Protein Chef bread is in the main Bread & Bakery aisle — directly next to Britannia (₹45), Harvest Gold (₹52), and English Oven (₹75). It's at eye level, but there's no 'protein bread' category signage — it just sits among regular breads with its ₹99 price tag sticking out. A regular shopper next to you picks up Protein Chef, looks at the price, puts it back and takes Britannia instead. What does this placement communicate about the brand — and would you pick it up here?

Scenario C: Lo! Foods has negotiated an end-cap display at the front of the health foods section — a standalone 'Protein Corner' branded unit showing all Lo! Foods products together with a QR code linking to recipes and nutritional information. A store employee is doing a 'protein bread tasting' nearby. The display includes a comparison chart: Protein Chef vs regular bread vs eggs for protein content. Does this branded in-store experience change your perception of Lo! Foods as a premium brand versus seeing it on a regular shelf?
rounds.decision: Option C — the dedicated branded end-cap — for both picking it up today AND maintaining premium brand perception in six months. Here's my reasoning: the end-cap is the only placement that allows Lo! Foods to control the complete value narrative. In the bread aisle, the brand gets defined by what it's next to — and it loses. In the health foods aisle, the brand gets defined by what it's next to — and it struggles. On its own end-cap, with the right visual communication, comparison data, and perhaps sampling, the brand defines itself. That's what I need. I need Lo! Foods to make the case to me directly. And six months from now, I'll still see it as premium because it's never been reduced to 'that expensive bread next to Britannia' — it has maintained its own identity and territory. Premium brand perception is about sustained distinctiveness, not just initial presentation. The end-cap protects that.
